text: The CT scan reveals a tumor located in the left hilum of the lung, adjacent to the inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon holds a sponge roll over the tumor, which is positioned near the descending aorta. The tumor is carefully separated from the aorta, allowing for some mobility. The surgeon dissects the inferior pulmonary vein, aiming to take it close to the left atrium for tumor margin. Both sides of the vein are dissected, focusing on the cephaloid side. The posterior mediastinal pleura is then dissected, with the anatomy showing the vein as the lowest structure, followed by the left lower lobe bronchus and the interlobar pulmonary artery above it.

The surgeon performs delicate dissection of the subcarinal lymph node located between the inferior pulmonary vein and the lower lobe bronchus, using close camera proximity to visualize the silver tips of the instruments. The subcarinal lymph node is removed to aid in isolating the inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon continues with medial hilar dissection, targeting nodes between the left upper and lower lobe bronchus. The superior pulmonary vein becomes visible as the surgeon examines the fissure, searching for the yellow hue of the pulmonary artery. A crossing vein is identified and clipped, revealing the underlying pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses medium clips to secure small branches of the pulmonary artery and vein, avoiding the use of bipolar cautery to prevent bleeding. The descending aorta is shifted leftward, complicating access to the inferior pulmonary vein. The surgeon employs an endolead catheter to guide the stapler safely behind the pulmonary vein, ensuring precise division without injury. The vein is successfully divided, and dissection proceeds on the bottom side of the left lower lobe bronchus, revealing the basilar and superior segmental bronchi adjacent to the pulmonary artery. A hilar lymph node, located between the left upper and lower lobe bronchi, is identified for removal.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to elevate the lung, exposing the space between the basilar segmental bronchi and the superior segmental bronchi. The dissection focuses on isolating the lower lobe bronchus, with the silver tips of the instruments visible as they separate the suprasegmental bronchus from the suprasegmental artery. The surgeon employs a tip-up instrument to safely traverse the left lower lobe bronchus, preparing to divide it using a green load stapler, while ensuring adequate space with a retrograde pull of the robnil catheter.

The surgeon dissects the space between the suprasegmental artery and the basilar trunk of the pulmonary artery. A small unnamed branch is clipped using two clips on the staying side, followed by division with hot scissors. The superior segmental branch is divided first using a gray load staple from the medial staple port, followed by the basilar trunk. The Rob Nell catheter aids in guiding the stapler through the tight space. The surgeon prepares to divide the fissure, ensuring the bronchial and artery stumps are positioned correctly outside the stapler.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to divide the thick fissure of the disproportionately large left lower lobe, ensuring the bronchial and artery stumps are below the staple line. The fourth arm is used to hold the tissue away, facilitating the stapling process. The lobe is then packed into an anchor bag for removal.
